location.name: Trevannick Cove, Cornwall
location.weather: iron frost and a cutting easterly off the sea

crime.cause_of_death: Poisoning by ingestion of powdered foxglove seed heads (digitalis)
crime.motive: Revenge for the deliberate death of her brother Thomas Polglase, who drowned more than twenty years ago when Josiah Carne withheld the signal lantern

characters.name: Josiah Carne
characters.description: A heavyset man of sixty-two with a florid complexion and large scarred hands, long the acknowledged captain of Trevannick's wrecking trade. The body is seated in a high-backed chair beside a low table, head lolling, the face locked in a rigid grimace, the lips and fingernails marked with a pronounced bluish tinge.

characters.name: Merryn Trevithick
characters.description: A hard-featured woman of forty-eight who runs the kitchen and bar of the Anchor and Tallow with precise, unhurried efficiency. She meets the investigator with dry eyes and a composure so complete it seems less like innocence than armour.

characters.name: Cador Trevithick
characters.description: The innkeeper, forty years old, broad-shouldered but soft around the eyes — a man who inherited his confidence from the bar trade and has been quietly losing it ever since. He greets the investigator with excessive helpfulness and a tremor in his left hand that he cannot seem to still.

characters.name: Tamsin Veal
characters.description: A dark-eyed young woman of twenty-eight who works as pot-girl at the inn, granddaughter to Elias Veal. Her hands are shaking when she carries the investigator's first cup of tea, and she sets it down harder than she intends.

characters.name: Elias Veal
characters.description: A lean, weathered man of fifty-five, Josiah Carne's lieutenant for thirty years, who now spends his days on the harbour wall watching the sea with the particular attention of a man who expects it to give something back. His expression when the investigator approaches is carefully neutral.

characters.name: Widow Nancarrow
characters.description: A small, upright woman of seventy who has lived above the cove for as long as anyone can remember, known for her knowledge of hedgerow plants and their uses. She was summoned by the constable before the investigator arrived and she receives the investigator without surprise, wiping her hands on a clean cloth.

characters.name: Constable Tredrea
characters.description: A square-jawed constable of thirty-five who sent for outside assistance the moment he found the body and has been visibly relieved by the investigator's arrival ever since. He has his notebook out before he is asked.

items.name: Pewter Toddy Cup
items.description: A battered pewter cup with a tin handle, containing a residue of dark liquid that smells strongly of rum and carries beneath it a faint sweet-sharp note quite distinct from the spirits.

items.name: Carved Wooden Token
items.description: A small flat disc of dark wood, no wider than a thumb, worn smooth on one face from long handling, with the letters T.P. carved shallowly into the reverse.

items.name: Copper Grinding Mortar
items.description: A small copper mortar with a matching pestle, set apart from other equipment on its own shelf, its interior filmed with a fine yellowish-green dust that has settled into the seam at the base.

items.name: Folded Letter
items.description: A folded sheet of paper, stiff with old frost and partially stuck to the ground, written in a close hand with the salutation 'my darling' and signed only with the initial M., the paper yellowed and the ink faded but fully legible.

items.name: Tin Oil Lamp
items.description: A small tin oil lamp with a recently trimmed wick, carrying a faint but distinct warmth and the smell of freshly burned oil when held close.

items.name: Salvage Ledger
items.description: A thick cloth-bound ledger whose pages are covered in coded entries and tallied columns, the last several pages torn cleanly away at the binding, leaving only short stubs of paper behind.

locations.name: The Anchor and Tallow Inn
locations.flavour: The only public house in the cove, its low beams black with forty years of tallow smoke, the bar already cleared and the fire banked low though it is barely mid-morning.

locations.name: The Inn Snug
locations.flavour: A narrow back room off the main bar, furnished with a single high-backed chair and a low table, the window unlatched and frost-rimed inside, the cold thick enough to see breath.

locations.name: The Drying Shed
locations.flavour: A small stone outbuilding behind the inn, its ceiling hung with bundles of dried coastal plants, the air carrying a sharp green smell that sits uneasily beneath the familiar scent of lavender and salt.

locations.name: The Harbour Wall
locations.flavour: A long curved arm of granite running into the sea, its stones glassed with spray-frost, the wind off the water sharp enough to make the eyes water.

locations.name: The Clifftop Path
locations.flavour: A narrow track cut into the cliff-top turf above the cove, the hawthorn on either side leafless and thorned black, the path itself hard-frozen and marked with yesterday's boot prints.

locations.name: The Wreckers' Storehouse
locations.flavour: A low granite building behind the cliff ridge, its floor scattered with old sawdust and the iron hooks and block-and-tackle of a salvage operation, one corner recently disturbed.

locations.name: Nancarrow's Cottage
locations.flavour: A single-storey whitewashed cottage at the top of the cliff path, its windows lit even in daylight, the doorstep worn smooth and bundles of dried plants visible through the glass.

checkpoints.label: When did Josiah Carne die?
answer_options: Before nine o'clock in the evening
Between nine and ten in the evening

checkpoints.label: Why was Josiah Carne killed?
answer_options: To protect a family secret about past wrecking
To prevent exposure of a romantic affair

clues.text: The body of [char:Josiah Carne] sits in the chair with lips and fingernails showing a deep bluish tinge, the face locked in a rigid grimace, the jaw clenched. On the table beside the body sits a pewter cup, and the smell that rises from it is rum first and underneath it something sweet-sharp and entirely unlike spirits.

[char:Widow Nancarrow] tells the investigator that when she examined the body at [char:Constable Tredrea]'s request, she noted a bitter-sweet smell on the breath quite separate from the rum, and that the hands were clenched in a manner she had seen once before in a man who had eaten hemlock berries by accident.
[char:Constable Tredrea] tells the investigator that when he found the body, he noticed a bruise on [char:Josiah Carne]'s right temple — a dark, purplish mark roughly the width of two fingers.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The bruise is a post-mortem injury sustained when Josiah fell from his chair as he died. It is not evidence of a blow.

clues.text: [char:Tamsin Veal] tells the investigator that [time:late in the evening], before she went upstairs, she heard sounds from the direction of the snug — a heavy struggling and a wet, rhythmic choking that she says sounded to her like a man fighting the sea.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Tamsin heard the death throes of Josiah's poisoning and described them in the only maritime terms available to her. The sounds were not caused by water.

clues.text: The window of the snug is unlatched and stands slightly open; frost has formed on the inside of the lower pane in a thick white crust, and the room is cold enough that the investigator's breath is visible throughout.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The window was left unlatched before the murder and is unrelated to the cause of death. The cold was present but did not kill Josiah.

clues.text: [char:Merryn Trevithick] tells the investigator that [char:Josiah Carne] came through the main bar and into the snug at [time:half past nine], as he did every night, and that she brought him his drink and left him there; she did not enter the snug again after that and did not hear him leave.

[char:Tamsin Veal] tells the investigator that she watched [char:Josiah Carne] walk into the snug himself at around [time:half past nine], carrying nothing, and that she heard the scrape of his chair as he settled — she did not hear that chair move again before she went upstairs.

[char:Elias Veal] tells the investigator that he saw [char:Josiah Carne] walking alone toward [loc:harbour_wall] at around [time:eight o'clock] the previous evening, which Elias considered unusual given the frost.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Josiah did walk to the harbour wall earlier in the evening on unrelated business but returned to the inn well before he was poisoned. The harbour wall was not the murder location.

clues.text: The frost on the [loc:clifftop_path] is broken by a set of large boot prints — a man's tread, by the size — running along the path toward the cliff edge and back again, the impressions still crisp and relatively fresh.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The boot prints belong to Constable Tredrea, who walked the clifftop that morning while looking for signs of disturbance. They have no connection to the murder.

clues.text: The [loc:wreckers_storehouse] shows a patch of recently disturbed sawdust near the door, and the tin oil lamp, when examined, carries warmth and the smell of burned oil that has not yet fully faded.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Cador Trevithick visited the storehouse that morning to remove pages from the salvage ledger before investigators arrived. The disturbance has no connection to the murder location.

clues.text: [char:Cador Trevithick] tells the investigator that the main bar closed at [time:half past nine] and that shortly before [time:a quarter to ten], he heard [char:Josiah Carne] knock the table in the snug — the signal Josiah always used when he wanted another drink brought through.

[char:Elias Veal] tells the investigator that he walked the [loc:harbour_wall] until [time:eleven o'clock] last night, as is his habit, and that the snug window of the inn was lit past [time:ten]; when he came back past the inn at [time:eleven], the window was dark.

clues.answer: Before nine o'clock in the evening
clues.text: [char:Tamsin Veal] tells the investigator that when she served [char:Josiah Carne] in the main bar at [time:around seven o'clock], his face was pale and there was visible perspiration on his forehead, which she had not seen in him before.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Josiah was nursing an ordinary winter cold that week and appeared unwell before the murder. The pallor and perspiration at seven o'clock were symptoms of his cold, not of poisoning.

clues.text: [char:Constable Tredrea] tells the investigator that [time:around two in the morning] he was woken by a dog howling in the lane outside the inn and lay awake for some minutes listening before it stopped.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The dog was disturbed by a fox in the lane and the howling had no connection to the death. Tredrea's sleeplessness at two o'clock is coincidental.

clues.answer: Between nine and ten in the evening
clues.text: [char:Cador Trevithick], when shown the pewter cup, tells the investigator that [char:Merryn Trevithick] always prepared [char:Josiah Carne]'s spiced toddy and brought it through to the snug immediately after bar-close, which was [time:half past nine] without exception.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The drink was indeed brought to the snug at half past nine, but Josiah did not die until later — the poison required time to act. Delivery of the drink and the moment of death are not the same.

clues.text: Set apart from the drying shed's other equipment on a shelf of its own, the copper mortar contains a fine yellowish-green dust packed into the seam at its base; on the shelf beside it lie several dried foxglove seed heads, two of them broken open, their contents partially emptied.

[char:Widow Nancarrow], when shown the copper mortar, identifies it without hesitation: she sold it to [char:Merryn Trevithick] approximately four months ago, and she recalls that on the day of purchase [char:Merryn Trevithick] asked her specifically about the seed heads on the dried foxglove bundles hanging in the workroom.

[char:Constable Tredrea] tells the investigator that [char:Cador Trevithick] owed [char:Josiah Carne] a significant sum from an old salvage-share arrangement, and that in the weeks before the death, [char:Josiah Carne] had been making public demands for repayment that caused visible friction between the two men.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The debt gives Cador an apparent motive, but he did not commit the murder. Merryn acted without his knowledge.

clues.text: [char:Elias Veal] tells the investigator, after some initial resistance, that he and [char:Josiah Carne] had a public falling-out two months ago over the division of the last salvage take, and that [char:Josiah Carne] had threatened to cut him out of future operations entirely.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Elias had genuine cause to resent Josiah and was present in the cove that night, but he did not kill him. His falling-out over salvage was real but unconnected to the murder.

clues.text: [char:Tamsin Veal] admits to the investigator that [time:two days before the death] she argued loudly with [char:Josiah Carne] in the main bar after he told her that her young man Abel Hicks would be run out of the cove if he continued to refuse the wrecking work.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Tamsin had strong reason to hate Josiah and the argument was real, but she did not poison him. Her hostility was open and recent, making her a plausible suspect who is ultimately innocent.

clues.text: [char:Elias Veal] tells the investigator, after a long silence, that on the night Thomas Polglase drowned in the cove more than twenty years ago, he saw [char:Josiah Carne] take the signal lantern from the post and put it in his coat — the signal that would have told Thomas it was safe to swim back from the wreck — and that Thomas Polglase was [char:Merryn Trevithick]'s younger brother.

[char:Merryn Trevithick]'s hands close around the carved token when the investigator presents it, and she tells him that it belonged to her brother Thomas, who drowned in the cove more than twenty years ago; her voice, steady through every prior question, becomes for the first time audibly uneven.

[char:Cador Trevithick] tells the investigator that [char:Josiah Carne] had come to him [time:the week before the death] and demanded full repayment of the salvage debt within the month, and that he had told [char:Cador Trevithick] plainly that failure to pay would have consequences for the inn.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The debt and the threat were real, and Cador had strong reason to want Josiah dead, but the motive for the actual murder was Merryn's long-nursed grief over her brother — not any financial calculation.

clues.answer: To protect a family secret about past wrecking
clues.text: The salvage ledger found in [loc:wreckers_storehouse] contains columns of coded entries going back more than thirty years, with a mark that repeats on the majority of pages; the final several pages have been torn away at the binding, leaving only short paper stubs.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Cador tore the pages that morning to conceal his own involvement in recent salvage operations before investigators arrived. The ledger records past wrecking but was not connected to the motive for Josiah's murder.

clues.answer: To prevent exposure of a romantic affair
clues.text: The folded letter found on the [loc:clifftop_path] is written in a close, intimate hand: it opens with 'my darling', speaks of meetings in secret and a fear that they will be discovered, and is signed only with the initial M.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The letter was written by Merryn to Cador during their courtship before their marriage, and was lost on the clifftop path years ago. The 'fear of discovery' refers to a courtship that Merryn's family disapproved of. It has no connection to the murder.

leads.text: Before the investigator arrived, [char:Constable Tredrea] sent for [char:Widow Nancarrow] from [loc:nancarrow_cottage] to look at the body — she is said to know more about the properties of hedgerow plants than any physician within twenty miles.
The padlock on the drying shed behind [loc:inn] was found hanging open this morning, and [char:Constable Tredrea] noted an unfamiliar sharp smell inside that he could not identify.
[char:Elias Veal] walks [loc:harbour_wall] every night regardless of weather and rarely sleeps before midnight — if any light was burning in the inn after closing time, he would have seen it.

opening_narrative:
The telegraph from Trevannick Cove reached the investigator on a Thursday morning in late January, relayed through two constabularies and finally delivered by a boy on a borrowed horse. The message was terse: a man found dead at the Anchor and Tallow Inn, circumstances not consistent with natural causes, outside assistance requested. The local constable, one [char:Constable Tredrea], had the reputation of a careful man in an impossible position — the cove policed itself, had always done so, and the dead man was [char:Josiah Carne], who had been the closest thing Trevannick had to a law of its own for thirty years.

The road down to the cove was a single track cut into the cliff face, glazed with frost and barely wide enough for the investigator's cart. The cove itself came into view below as a narrow cleft in the granite, a shingle beach at its foot and a scatter of stone buildings above it, the whole scene iron-grey and still under a sky the colour of old pewter. The [loc:inn] was the largest building in the cove, its sign — an anchor and a tallow candle, both rendered in flaking paint — the first thing visible on the descent.

[char:Constable Tredrea] was waiting at the door of the [loc:inn] when the investigator arrived, his breath visible in the cold air, his expression that of a man who has been waiting some hours and is not certain whether to be relieved or apprehensive. He reported that [char:Josiah Carne] had been found in the inn's private snug [time:that morning] when the inn failed to open at its customary hour, and that the body had not been disturbed since discovery. He had sent for [char:Widow Nancarrow] to look at the body first, as there was no physician in the cove, and she had told him only that the smell was wrong before declining to say more until someone with authority arrived.
epilogue:
The case against [char:Merryn Trevithick] rested on the grinding mortar and [char:Widow Nancarrow]'s testimony, and when [char:Elias Veal] finally gave his account of the night Thomas Polglase drowned, it collapsed the last pretence of doubt. Merryn said nothing at her arrest that she had not already said. She was taken to Bodmin in a closed wagon on a grey February morning, and [char:Cador Trevithick] stood in the doorway of the [loc:inn] and watched until the road bent and she was gone from sight. He did not speak of her again, not to anyone who asked.

The [loc:inn] continued under Cador's management, though the salvage debt was eventually called in by Josiah's estate and settled at considerable cost. [char:Tamsin Veal] remained as pot-girl for the remainder of that winter and left in the spring to marry Abel Hicks and move to Falmouth, where Josiah's influence did not reach. [char:Elias Veal] stayed on the [loc:harbour_wall] until the following autumn, when he was found one morning seated on the granite with his eyes open and the sea in front of him, gone quietly in the night; the doctor said his heart had simply stopped. Whether it was relief or grief or merely the cold, no one could say.

[char:Widow Nancarrow] returned to [loc:nancarrow_cottage] after giving her testimony and was not called to court. She removed the dried foxglove from her workroom that same week and did not replace it. The carved token was never entered into evidence — [char:Merryn Trevithick] had asked for it back and been refused, and it remained in an evidence drawer in Bodmin for years, worn smooth on one face from a handling that had lasted more than twenty years. Thomas Polglase's name, meanwhile, appeared in no official record of the proceedings; his drowning had long since been recorded as accidental, and no one moved to change it.
